policy_statement: Undertake a gap analysis of existing communication processes related to climate change and extreme weather.
mechanism_description: Conducting a gap analysis of climate and extreme-weather communication processes identifies weaknesses and informs improvements that strengthen institutional adaptive capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Institutional process improvement → ↑ risk communication effectiveness → ↓ climate hazard impacts (via ↓ exposure/↓ vulnerability)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human activity/system changed: Government/institutional communication protocols, roles, channels, and procedures for climate change and extreme-weather information (e.g., warnings, internal coordination, public messaging) are assessed and redesigned based on identified gaps. (2) Mediating process: Better information flow, clearer responsibilities, faster dissemination, and improved message consistency increase decision quality and timeliness before/during events. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced exposure and vulnerability to climate hazards (e.g., fewer injuries/damages, faster recovery), reflecting increased institutional adaptive capacity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved preparedness and response to climate hazards through stronger risk communication, reducing impacts and vulnerability.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: the mechanism strengthens institutional capacity to manage climate and extreme-weather risks through improved communication processes, leading to reduced hazard impacts. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: while better communication can indirectly support mitigation programs, the dominant and intended climate effect is reducing vulnerability and improving response—an adaptation outcome. 3) Edge cases applied: if “communication” were primarily about promoting low-carbon behaviors, it could be mitigation; here it is explicitly tied to extreme weather and adaptive capacity, so adaptation dominates. 4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy using “communication gap analysis → institutional adaptive capacity” principally affects preparedness/response capacity and thus fits Adaptation. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., emergency management vs. public health vs. infrastructure resilience) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the gap analysis is repurposed mainly for climate-action communications (e.g., emissions-reduction campaigns) rather than extreme-weather risk communication, the dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation; otherwise this is straightforward Adaptation.
key_indicators: "Undertake a gap analysis"; "existing communication processes"; "related to climate change and extreme weather"
co_benefits: Improved general emergency communications and institutional coordination for non-climate hazards.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as “information-disclosure” if the gap analysis were explicitly published for public accountability; the text only specifies conducting the analysis, so it is treated as an internal planning/process-improvement measure.
